docs.title: Restore
docs.text: Restore uploads emails from your local SQLite database back to an IMAP server. It preserves the original mailbox structure, flags (read/unread), and dates.

docs.title: Why Restore?
docs.text:
• Migration — move emails between servers (Hostinger to Gmail, Gmail to Outlook, etc.)
  • Disaster recovery — re-upload emails after a server failure
  • Account merge — consolidate multiple accounts into one
  • Testing — populate a test server with real data

docs.title: Restore All Mailboxes
docs.text:
Each email is uploaded to its original mailbox. If a mailbox doesn't exist on the target server, it's created automatically.

Python 3.8+ 
# Code above omitted 👆

        # Restore all mailboxes to server
        count = app.restore()
        print(f"Restored {count} emails")

# Code below omitted 👇
👀 Full file preview 
"""Sync and restore emails."""

from email_profile import Email


def main() -> None:
    with Email.from_env() as app:
        # Sync all mailboxes
        result = app.sync()
        print(f"Synced: {result.inserted} new, {result.skipped} skipped")

        # Sync one mailbox
        result = app.sync(mailbox="INBOX")
        print(f"INBOX: {result.inserted} new")

        # Restore all mailboxes to server
        count = app.restore()
        print(f"Restored {count} emails")

        # Restore one mailbox
        count = app.restore(mailbox="INBOX")
        print(f"Restored {count} to INBOX")

        # Restore without duplicate check (faster)
        count = app.restore(skip_duplicates=False)

        # Control parallelism
        result = app.sync(max_workers=5)
        count = app.restore(max_workers=5)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

docs.title: Skip Duplicates
docs.text:
By default, restore checks if an email already exists on the server (by Message-ID) and skips it. Disable this for faster restores when you know the server is empty:

count = app.restore(skip_duplicates=False)

docs.title: Parallel Workers
docs.text:
Like sync, restore uses threads — one per mailbox:

count = app.restore(max_workers=5)

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
Each mailbox shows a progress bar while uploading:

  • uploaded — emails sent to the server
  • skipped — emails already on the server (duplicate detection)

docs.title: Restore to a Different Server
docs.text:
Sync from one server, restore to another:

from email_profile import Email, StorageSQLite

storage = StorageSQLite("./backup.db")

# Step 1: sync from source server
with Email("user@old-server.com", "password") as source:
    source.sync()

# Step 2: restore to target server
with Email("user@new-server.com", "password", storage=storage) as target:
    count = target.restore()
    print(f"Migrated {count} emails")

docs.title: What gets restored?
docs.text: Data Restored? Email content (RFC822) Yes Mailbox/folder Yes (created if missing) Flags (read/unread, flagged) Yes Original date Yes (from Date header) IMAP UID No (new UID assigned by server)

"""Send emails via SMTP."""

from email_profile import Email


def main() -> None:
    with Email.from_env() as app:
        # Simple send
        app.send(
            to="recipient@example.com",
            subject="Hello",
            body="Hi there!",
        )

        # With HTML
        app.send(
            to="recipient@example.com",
            subject="Report",
            body="See attached report.",
            html="<h1>Report</h1><p>See attached.</p>",
        )

        # With attachments
        app.send(
            to=["alice@x.com", "bob@x.com"],
            subject="Files",
            body="Here are the files.",
            attachments=["report.pdf", "data.csv"],
        )

        # With CC/BCC
        app.send(
            to="recipient@example.com",
            subject="Meeting",
            body="Tomorrow at 10am.",
            cc="manager@example.com",
            bcc="hr@example.com",
        )

        # Reply to an email
        msg = list(app.inbox.where().messages(chunk_size=1))[0]
        app.reply(msg, body="Thanks for your email!")

        # Forward an email
        app.forward(msg, to="colleague@example.com", body="FYI")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

docs.title: Sync
docs.text: Sync downloads emails from your IMAP server to a local SQLite database. It's incremental — only new emails are downloaded. Already synced emails are skipped based on their IMAP UID.

docs.title: Why Sync?
docs.text:
• Backup — keep a local copy of all your emails in case the server goes down
  • Offline access — read and search emails without an internet connection
  • Migration — download from one server, restore to another
  • Analysis — query your emails with SQL directly on the SQLite file

        # Sync all mailboxes
        result = app.sync()
        print(f"Synced: {result.inserted} new, {result.skipped} skipped")

        # Sync one mailbox
        result = app.sync(mailbox="INBOX")
        print(f"INBOX: {result.inserted} new")

        # Restore all mailboxes to server
        count = app.restore()
        print(f"Restored {count} emails")

        # Restore one mailbox
        count = app.restore(mailbox="INBOX")
        print(f"Restored {count} to INBOX")

        # Restore without duplicate check (faster)
        count = app.restore(skip_duplicates=False)

        # Control parallelism
        result = app.sync(max_workers=5)
        count = app.restore(max_workers=5)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

"""Sync and restore emails."""

from email_profile import Email


def main() -> None:
    with Email.from_env() as app:
        # Sync all mailboxes
        result = app.sync()
        print(f"Synced: {result.inserted} new, {result.skipped} skipped")

        # Sync one mailbox
        result = app.sync(mailbox="INBOX")
        print(f"INBOX: {result.inserted} new")

        # Restore all mailboxes to server
        count = app.restore()
        print(f"Restored {count} emails")

        # Restore one mailbox
        count = app.restore(mailbox="INBOX")
        print(f"Restored {count} to INBOX")

        # Restore without duplicate check (faster)
        count = app.restore(skip_duplicates=False)

        # Control parallelism
        result = app.sync(max_workers=5)

docs.title: Parallel Workers
docs.text:
By default, sync uses 3 threads — one per mailbox. You can increase for faster syncs or decrease if the server limits connections.

result = app.sync(max_workers=5)

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
Each mailbox shows a progress bar while downloading. When complete, it prints a summary:

  • new — emails downloaded and saved for the first time
  • updated — emails that existed but were re-saved (e.g. UID changed)
  • skipped — emails already in the database

docs.title: SyncResult
docs.text:
The sync() method returns a SyncResult with all the counts:

result = app.sync()

print(result.inserted)
print(result.updated)
print(result.skipped)
print(result.errors)
print(result.total_processed)
print(result.has_errors)

docs.title: Where are emails stored?
docs.text:
By default, in ./email.db (SQLite). You can change this:

from email_profile import Email, StorageSQLite

with Email.from_env(storage=StorageSQLite("./backup.db")) as app:
    app.sync()

docs.title: How to see the data?
docs.text:
Open the SQLite file with any tool:

sqlite3 email.db "SELECT count(*) FROM raw"
sqlite3 email.db "SELECT uid, mailbox, message_id FROM raw LIMIT 10"
sqlite3 email.db "SELECT mailbox, count(*) FROM raw GROUP BY mailbox"

Or with Python:

from email_profile import StorageSQLite

storage = StorageSQLite("./email.db")
print(f"Total: {len(storage.ids())}")
print(f"INBOX: {len(storage.uids('INBOX'))}")
